section_title: INTRODUCTION TO IMPLEMENTING THE ASIAN RANGE
content:
This teaching specifically deals with implementing the Asian Range. It's important to note that the Asian
Range concept was not created or authored by me (ICT); my first introduction to it was from another trader,
Chris Laurie. This module will introduce the Asian Range, define what it is in charts, and teach how to
utilize it in both bullish and bearish conditions.
Key areas to be covered:
- Introducing the Asian Range.
- Defining the Asian Range in charts.
- Utilizing it in bullish conditions.
- Utilizing it in bearish conditions.

section_title: DEFINING THE ASIAN RANGE AND ITS ORIGINS
content:
The Asian Range is characterized by price action prior to the Frankfurt or London open, which can be very
indicative of future intraday price movement. When you have a directional bias, you can use this Asian
Range to build or frame a context or storyline to the market's likely intentions. There is often a stillness in
price many times right before the intraday directional impulse price swing.
On a chart (e.g., Aussie dollar), the Asian Range is a shaded area, a gray shaded box, delineating this
specific piece of price action. I learned about this from Chris Laurie (ChrisLaurie.com). While my initial
understanding of price behavior was already developing, seeing his reference to the Asian Range in his
free teachings provided immediate context.

section_title: THE UTILITY OF CONSOLIDATION AND CONTEXT
content:
I appreciated the Asian Range concept because I was already familiar with using consolidations in certain
periods of a 24-hour trading cycle. Taking certain ranges can lead to 'magical' things, as taught in the
mentorship, going beyond what has been shared in YouTube videos.
My primary takeaway from Chris Laurie's material was the immediate context provided by the Asian Range.
I will share my previous entry techniques and how I incorporated this concept, though I am not revealing
everything I know about its implementation over the last ten years. For a developing trader, the
implementation is useful, but it must have context. The range itself doesn't produce anything magical; you
need a storyline behind what price should be doing that particular day for it to be unbelievably helpful.

section_title: TIME AND DIMENSIONS
content:
To define the Asian Range in your charts:
Time Parameters:
- The range begins at 7:00 p.m. New York time.
- It ends at midnight New York time.
Special Inclusion: Chris Laurie's Asian Range sometimes extended beyond midnight, possibly to 12:30
a.m. However, I believe the financial markets truly begin at midnight New York time, especially for intraday
price action, as this sets the algorithm's reset.
Dimensional Parameters:
- The height of the range is the highest high between 7:00 p.m. to midnight New York time to the lowest low
between 7:00 p.m. and midnight New York time.
- The width of the range is the duration of 7:00 p.m. to midnight New York time.

section_title: CHARTING THE ASIAN RANGE MANUALLY
content:
When charting, your focus every single day should be zeroed in on 7:00 p.m. New York time. Identify this
time on your broker's platform or charting package, then:
- Place a vertical line at 7:00 p.m. New York time to begin the Asian session.
- Five hours later, at midnight New York time, place another vertical line.
- The highest high and lowest low within this duration define the Asian Range.
There's a lot of 'voodoo' you can do with this range, which can lead to capturing the highest and lowest
points of the day, but it requires incorporating other elements taught in the mentorship. For now, we'll teach
a simple approach to use it and filter out trades that may lead to losses.

section_title: MARKET MECHANICS WITHIN THE ASIAN RANGE
content:
My interpretation of what's happening during this range is that orders are stacking up above and below the
market, relative to that range. This also builds market sentiment. This compression of market participation
builds up closer and closer until midnight in New York.
As soon as we cross over midnight in New York, we are in fertile ground for price movement. If you're
familiar with channels or trading ranges, the idea is that above this range, there will be buy orders, and
below this range, there will be sell orders.

section_title: LIQUIDITY AND TRADER TRAPS IN THE ASIAN RANGE
content:
Breakout traders, who buy on strength, will place buy orders above the Asian Range. They'll buy a breakout
above a known high and place their stop-loss right below the low of the range.
Short sellers will do the opposite: they'll have their sell orders below the range, hoping for a short position
on a break lower, and place their protective buy stop at the other end of the range, above the high.
- Buyers want to break out above this consolidation, buying on a stop with a protective sell stop below the
low.
- Short sellers want to sell on a stop and use a protective buy stop above the high.
The storyline is: what will price do after midnight New York time (the vertical line)? My career has been
based on understanding this phenomenon which side will 'they' work on? Will they try to put a specific
party of traders (bullish or bearish) on the wrong side right before London creates the higher or lower of the
day?

section_title: THE NARROW ASIAN RANGE AND TRENDING MODELS
content:
The key takeaway, which should be in your notes, is that a very narrow consolidated range between 7:00
p.m. and midnight (the Asian Range) sets up a huge possibility of the algorithm entering a trending model.
In other words, the market will seek the liquidity above or below this range with the premise that it's going
to put traders on the wrong side, and then it'll go the other way for the remainder of the trading day.

section_title: UTILIZING THE ASIAN RANGE IN BULLISH CONDITIONS
content:
- INTRODUCTION
In periods when price is bullish, we can extend the Asian Range high and low into the future. When price
returns back to the Asian Range high, we can anticipate institutional buying.
For example, assume the Aussie dollar was bullish for a particular day. This bullish expectation could be
derived from higher timeframes; a daily chart might show it reaching an equal high, and a four-hour or one
- hour chart would make the liquidity targets obvious.

section_title: PRE-ASIAN RANGE TRADING METHOD (ICT'S ORIGINAL APPROACH)
content:
Before my exposure to the Asian Range, this is what I used to do, dating back to 1994. This method formed
the basis for my current concepts of 'buying below the open when it's bullish' or 'selling above the open
when it's bearish.'
My method involved:
- Identifying the opening price at midnight New York time.
- Drawing that opening price line out until 11:00 a.m. New York time (the end of the morning trend for the
S&P 500, aligning with the London close scenario).
- For a bullish scenario, I would look for price to initially drop below that opening price, as I wanted to buy
below the opening price.

section_title: ICT'S EVOLVED BULLISH ENTRY STRATEGY (PRE-ASIAN RANGE)
content:
My interpretation of how to 'buy below the open' involved waiting for price to drop below the midnight
opening price. I would try to buy it there. If I missed the opportunity or didn't have the conviction, I would
look at the high right before the drop. There would often be a small segment of price action before the drop,
and I would place a buy stop right there.
As my understanding evolved, I would aim to enter as price was dropping, but I'd also have this buy stop in
place. Many times, price would trigger both my market order (as it dropped) and my buy stop, filling half the
position I wanted to trade at the buy stop level. For instance, if my full position was ten standard lots, I'd
have a five-standard-lot buy stop.
This ensured I got at least half a position. Risk management involved risking 1% to 1.5% per trade. If both
positions filled, I'd aggressively move my initial stop-loss up to keep the total risk around 2%.

section_title: BULLISH EXAMPLE
content:
Contrasting my old method with the Asian Range, this is exactly where price would fill you with that buy
stop being tripped. If I didn't get filled below the opening price at midnight, this would be where my buy stop
would activate. My stop loss would be below the low, and I would have to weather a retracement before
seeing the expected move later in the day.

section_title: THE ASIAN RANGE AND ALGORITHM RESET IN BULLISH CONDITIONS
content:
My clearer view on price action emerged using the Asian Range, defined from 7:00 p.m. New York time to
exactly midnight (not 12:30 or 1:00 a.m.). We are banking on the midnight opening price, which resets the
Interbank Price Delivery Algorithm (HIPPA). If we're bullish, the algorithm will go below that opening price
to seek liquidity, then move higher and spend the rest of the day going higher. If bearish, it will go above the
opening price at midnight to reach for liquidity and then move lower.
When we have a tight Asian Range and anticipate bullishness, price right after midnight might go up and
tap the Asian Range high. Even if it doesn't visibly go above it on your chart, the broker's spread will often
open wide enough to trip buy stops there, putting you long, and then your stop-loss (placed below the low)
will be tagged. This is why a stop loss of anything less than 10 pips is ludicrous; you're inviting the retail
broker to take your stop.

section_title: BULLISH ASIAN RANGE APPLICATION
content:
- LIQUIDITY SWEEPS
In a bullish scenario with an Asian Range:
- Price initially goes up, then comes back down and breaks the Asian Range low.
- This move below the extended Asian Range low trips in sellers and also knocks out individuals who would
have been pulled in on a buy stop at the high.
- Long holders are in and then out; short holders are in and their stop-loss is above the high. The market is
taking both sides in and out, then it takes off and goes higher.

section_title: OPTIMAL ENTRY IN BULLISH ASIAN RANGE CONDITIONS
content:
What we look for is the movement that sets the tone. When we're bullish, we want to be buying there.
Alternatively, you can place a buy stop at the Asian Range high. If it gets filled, a contingent order (e.g., if
filled with your broker for a buy on a stop) would then place a sell stop below the low at a specific price
level. You could leave that in the marketplace and not worry about watching the action.
If you miss this initial opportunity and price rallies away, you must have the bullish storyline behind the
example. Otherwise, this could easily be a selling opportunity. Knowing where the market is most likely
trying to go from a higher time frame standpoint is significant and crucial for utilizing the Asian Range
effectively, or you will perform unprofitably.

section_title: CONFIRMATION AND HIGHER TIME FRAME BIAS
content:
If we're bullish, we want to see the market drop below the Asian Range low and then trade back up to the
Asian Range high. We don't need to capture the initial low. We would wait for price to come back down and
touch the Asian Range high. This might happen early, or it could happen again in the New York open.
When price trades back to the Asian Range high during the New York open, that is where institutional
buying is going to step in.
Why? Because the initial range set between 7:00 p.m. and midnight is reset at midnight by HIPPA, the
interbank price delivery algorithm. It then attacks open liquidity or the open flow, which would be the order
table above and below the Asian Range.

section_title: INTERBANK PRICE DELIVERY ALGORITHM AND ASIAN RANGE
content:
While some in the banking world might dismiss this explanation as nonsense, I tell you it's based on
consistent observation of price action, day after day, week after week. What I'm suggesting is observable in
price action, even if it falls into a 'tinfoil hat' category for some. We can see the optimal trade entry from this
low to this high, retracing back down, coinciding with the New York open and the Asian Range high. This is
a beautiful illustration of an Asian Range application in bullish conditions, leading to a strong move.
We have two opportunities in the New York open and one setup for a very low entry point with minimal risk.

section_title: BULLISH ASIAN RANGE OPPORTUNITIES AND RULES
content:
Another entry opportunity would be entering on a stop above the Asian Range high. However, you must
wait for price to drop down below the Asian Range low first. Then, and only then, can a buy stop be placed
at the Asian Range high.
Critical Rules for Bullish Buy Stop:
- Conditions must be bullish.
- You must first see the Asian Range low break.
- Then a buy stop can be placed at the Asian Range high.
If you place a buy stop before the Asian Range low is taken, you will get burned and stopped out. These
rules are very clear.

section_title: UTILIZING THE ASIAN RANGE IN BEARISH CONDITIONS
content:
- INTRODUCTION
Similar to bullish conditions, when it's bearish, there's a quiet period right before the Frankfurt and London
open, which is our focus. If this period shows a tight, narrow consolidation, we anticipate some measure of
manipulation in price action.
In bearish conditions, we wait for a break in the Asian Range high. Then, when price trades back down to
it, we anticipate institutional selling.

section_title: PRE-ASIAN RANGE TRADING METHOD (BEARISH
content:
- ICT'S ORIGINAL APPROACH)
Prior to learning about the Asian Range, for bearish scenarios, I would:
- Identify the midnight opening price.
- Assume a bearish bias for the currency.
- Look for price movement above the opening price. I learned early that double tops were often fake outs,
which led me to appreciate the 'Turtle Soup' trading pattern from the book 'Street Smarts.' This pattern
resonated because I was already looking for equal highs and lows.
If it was midnight and we were bearish, and there were equal highs, I knew there would be a rally above
the opening price. I'd extend that opening price line to about 11:00 a.m. New York time. Any time price
traded above that opening price within a 'kill zone' (London or New York open), I would look to go short.

section_title: ICT'S EVOLVED BEARISH ENTRY STRATEGY (PRE-ASIAN RANGE)
content:
If I saw a rally above the opening price, I would sell short right there (e.g., S&P, Deutschmark, Swiss Franc,
commodity futures). That was my entry technique. I would then go to sleep, wake up around 5:00 a.m., take
some profit, and move my stop-loss very tight. Sometimes I'd get stopped out, other times it would carry
into Globex and open outcry, leading to more follow-through in the New York session.
If I couldn't be awake, I'd place a limit order about two pips above the equal highs, with a stop-loss of 35 to
40 pips. My take-profit would be 80 pips (5 pips over my weekly objective of 75 pips). My objective is 50-75
pips weekly, after which I stop trading. I don't trail stops; I manage risk by taking partials and slowly moving
the stop-loss down after New York trading starts, especially if expecting a large daily range.
If I missed the initial selling opportunity, my eyes would go to the low that formed prior to that rally above
the opening price. I would place a sell stop there to get in. If this order (contingent on filling) failed, it would
place a protective buy stop above the high plus 3 pips.

section_title: LIQUIDITY SWEEPS
content:
Bringing the conversation back to the Asian Range, in bearish conditions with a tight, narrow consolidation:
- Buyers wanting to buy on the breakout get tripped in with a move above the Asian Range high. They are
now long, and their stop-loss is below the low.
- The market then drives right down, conveniently 10 pips below that low, sweeping out anyone with a stop
- loss at or just below it. Traders using a small tail or wick for their stop loss think they're safe, but they're not.
- The real move involves running above the Asian Range high after the buy stops have been tripped, then
those positions taken out. Price is then driven back up again because short sellers who managed to sell at
the top of the channel are now profitable. The algorithm runs their stops (buy stops above these highs),
providing the optimal point to be a seller.

section_title: OPTIMAL ENTRY IN BEARISH ASIAN RANGE CONDITIONS AND RULES
content:
This move (running above the Asian Range high to trip buy stops) is the lowest-risk, high-probability entry
for using the Asian Range in bearish conditions, especially if you have the outlined entry strategy and a
clear storyline. You must have a bearish bias for the day and not change gears based on small movements.
- Primary Entry: Sell above the Asian Range high when bearish, especially after seeing the initial sweep
below the low.
- Low-Risk Confirmation Trade: Wait for the Asian Range low to be broken, then wait for price to trade back
up to it, forming resistance, and sell there.
If these opportunities occur during an ICT Kill Zone (London Open or New York Open), the probabilities of
success go 'through the roof.' You will see this unfold in your charts if you analyze them with this in mind.

section_title: THE IMPORTANCE OF MANUAL CHARTING AND BIAS
content:
Instead of relying on indicators, physically draw the Asian Range levels on your charts. Spend time getting
intimate with price action; it will teach you much more. I had to do this with hand-drawn charts, rulers, and
highlighters, and I'm convinced that's what made me proficient. Being 'lazy' about this means you don't
have time to truly study, and you won't gain the same level of appreciation or understanding if you shortcut
it.
Take the time to draw these levels manually and even type out notations in the chart: for example, when
bearish, 'do not focus on movements below the Asian Range; focus primarily on a move above the Asian
Range high, and/or if the low breaks after midnight, wait for a retest to confirm resistance.'
Understanding tape reading, building context behind price, and sticking with a bias are crucial. Anyone who
says 'don't trade with a bias' likely doesn't know how to do it. While a daily bias won't be 100% accurate,
you can achieve upper 90s in probability. If you can achieve 65-70% accuracy with your bias and then wait
for conditions like these, adding time of day, kill zones, and focusing on the Asian Range, it builds the entire
model for the daily range. You won't be surprised; you'll anticipate price action. This consistent practice
leads to better interpretation and forecasting, a skill learned through dedicated chart time, not just books or
videos.
